mechanism_narrative: A low unemployment rate (UNRATE at 4.2%) indicates a labor market operating beyond full employment, which through wage-push inflation channels lifts inflation expectations and forces bond investors to demand higher term premiums, resulting in an upward move in the 10-year yield over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: If the rank correlation between UNRATE level and subsequent 20-day yield change is not significantly positive (one-tailed p>0.10) over any 3-year rolling window, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: An inflation index (CPIAUCSL) above 250 triggers market expectations of earlier or faster monetary tightening by the Federal Reserve. Higher expected policy rates mechanically increase term premiums on long-term bonds, pushing the 10-year yield upward over the following 20 trading days.
failure_condition: If in instances where CPIAUCSL exceeds 250, the 20-day yield change is not positive in at least 60% of cases over any 2-year period, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production (INDPRO) declines below 100 and the federal funds rate (FEDFUNDS) remains below 2, the market interprets this as a supply-side contraction met with accommodative policy. This combination signals a deeper economic slowdown, reducing the neutral real rate and pushing long-term yields down over the next 20 trading days.
regime_hint: economic slowdown with accommodative monetary policy
failure_condition: If in periods where both conditions hold, the subsequent 20-day yield change is not negative in at least 55% of instances over any 2-year window, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: During an economic expansion (UNRATE below 5%), high levels of nonfarm payrolls (PAYEMS) reinforce expectations of sustained demand and wage inflation. This prompts the bond market to price in higher future policy rates, causing the 10-year yield to rise over the next 5 trading days.
failure_condition: If the average 5-day yield change following a PAYEMS reading above 146,000 is not positive (t-test p<0.10) in expansion periods over the past 10 years, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: Housing starts (HOUST) above the 1.2 million mark signal robust housing demand that lifts construction activity, household wealth, and inflation expectations. This demand-side pressure leads to a higher neutral interest rate, driving the 10-year yield upward over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: If the probability of a 60-day yield increase given HOUST>1200 is less than 0.6 in a 5-year backtest, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: Retail sales (RSAFS) at elevated levels (above 480,000) indicate persistent consumer spending that sustains GDP growth and keeps inflation elevated. The bond market therefore demands higher yields to compensate for inflation risk and expected Fed tightening, with the effect materializing over the next 60 trading days.
failure_condition: If the rank correlation between RSAFS level and subsequent 60-day yield change is not positive (IC<0.02) over any 3-year window, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: In a low-unemployment regime (UNRATE below 5%), above-trend real GDP (GDPC1) signals an overheating economy that will require restrictive monetary policy. This expectation of tighter policy elevates the entire yield curve, pushing the 10-year yield higher over the next 20 trading days.
failure_condition: If the average 20-day yield change following GDPC1 releases above 17000 is not positive (t-test p<0.10) when UNRATE is below 5%, reject the hypothesis.

mechanism_narrative: When industrial production (INDPRO) exceeds 105, the market interprets this as a supply-side glut that alleviates cost pressures and inflation. This disinflationary supply shock reduces term premiums and pushes the 10-year yield downward over the next 5 trading days, as investors rotate into bonds.
failure_condition: If in cases where INDPRO exceeds 105, the 5-day yield change is not negative in at least 55% of instances over a 3-year walk-forward, reject the hypothesis.
